<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سوغا (عشيرة)]][[ca:Clan Soga]][[de:Soga (Klan)]][[en:Soga clan]][[es:Clan Soga]][[fa:خاندان سوگا]][[fi:Soga-klaani]][[fr:Clan Soga]][[hr:Soga (klan)]][[hu:Szoga klán]][[id:Klan Soga]][[it:Clan Soga]][[ja:蘇我氏]][[ko:소가씨]][[nl:Soga]][[pl:Ród Soga]][[pt:Clã Soga]][[ru:Сога (род)]][[sh:Soga (klan)]][[tr:Soga boyu]][[uk:Рід Соґа]][[vi:Gia tộc Soga]][[zh:蘇我氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q909854]] Wikidata name: Soga clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2023}} {{Short description|Aristocratic family (uji) in Yamato-period Japan (3rd-8th centuries)}} {{Distinguish |Soga clan (Sagami Province)}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 蘇我氏 |home province =[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] |titles =Various |parent house = [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] |founder = Soga no Ishikawa |final ruler =[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] |dissolution =645 |ruled until =645, [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]] |cadet branches =Ishikawa clan }} The {{nihongo|'''Soga clan'''|{{linktext|蘇我|氏}}|Soga uji|lead=yes}} was one of the most powerful aristocratic kin groups [[Uji (clan)|(''uji'')]] of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] of the early Japanese state—the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato polity]]—and played a major role in the spread of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. Through the 5th and 7th centuries, the Soga monopolized the [[kabane]] or hereditary Rank of Great [[Omi (title)|Omi]] and was the first of many families to dominate the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] by influencing the order of succession and government policy.
The last Soga predates any historical work in Japan, and very little is known about its earliest members.
==Origins== The Soga clan is believed to have been founded by [[Soga no Ishikawa]], a great-grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]].
==Notation== Today, the name ''Soga'', when referring to the Soga clan, is written in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] as 蘇我. This notation derives from the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', where 蘇我 is the principal way in which this name is written.<ref name="nihonshoki-wiki">{{cite web |url=http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208102258/http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |title=Soga |archive-date=December 8, 2019 |website=The Nihon Shoki Wiki |accessdate=2023-07-01 |lang=en}}</ref> Other ways of writing the clan name appeared in other historical documents.<ref name="Soga-Japanese">[[:ja:蘇我氏|Soga clan]], Japanese Wikipedia.</ref> The two characters used in this name are [[:en:Ateji|Ateji]]; the meanings of the characters (蘇: "resuscitation"; 我: "self") are unrelated to the name meaning.
==Soga no Iname== [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] served as Great Minister from 536 until his death in 570, and was the first of the Soga clan to carry to extreme lengths the domination of the Throne by the nobility. One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}
==Family== *[[Takenouchi no Sukune]] (武内宿禰, ?–?) **[[Soga no Ishikawa]] (蘇我石川, ?–?) ***[[Soga no Machi]] (蘇我満智, ?–?) ****[[Soga no Karako]] (蘇我韓子, ?–465) *****[[:en:Soga no Koma|Soga no Koma]] (蘇我高麗, ?–?) ******[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] (蘇我稲目, c.506–570) *******[[Soga no Umako]] (蘇我馬子, 551?–626) ********[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] (蘇我蝦夷, 587–645) *********[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] (蘇我入鹿, 610?–645) ********[[Soga no Zentoko]] (蘇我善徳, ?–?) ********[[Soga no Kuramaro]] (蘇我倉麻呂, ?–?) *********[[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro]] (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂, ?–649) *********[[Soga no Akae]] (蘇我赤兄, 623?–?) *********[[Soga no Murajiko]] (蘇我連子, 611?–664) **********[[Soga no Yasumaro]] (蘇我安麻呂, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Himuka]] (蘇我日向, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Hatayasu]] (蘇我果安, ?–672)
==References== {{Reflist}} *Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Hall, John Whitney, et al. (1993). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: Volume 1 Ancient Japan''. Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}.]]
{{draft categories| [[Category:Soga clan| ]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]]
[[Category:Soga clan]]
}}
[[en:Soga clan]]
<!-- Bot generated title --><!-- A Japan Times article would not be the best source in the world for this -- they have published articles that, for instance, misspelled Nakanishi Susumu's name and called the Man'youshuu Japanese "oldest collection of domestic poetry" -- but the fact that what is actually linked is a blog, even one by the excellent Aileen Kawagoe, is concerning. ~Hijiri88, August 2015. -->

the spread of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. Through the 5th and 7th centuries, the Soga monopolized the [[kabane]] or hereditary Rank of Great [[Omi (title)|Omi]] and was the first of many families to dominate the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] by influencing the order of succession and government policy.
The last Soga predates any historical work in Japan, and very little is known about its earliest members.
==Origins== The Soga clan is believed to have been founded by [[Soga no Ishikawa]], a great-grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]].
==Notation== Today, the name ''Soga'', when referring to the Soga clan, is written in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] as 蘇我. This notation derives from the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', where 蘇我 is the principal way in which this name is written.<ref name="nihonshoki-wiki">{{cite web |url=http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208102258/http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |title=Soga |archive-date=December 8, 2019 |website=The Nihon Shoki Wiki |accessdate=2023-07-01 |lang=en}}</ref> Other ways of writing the clan name appeared in other historical documents.<ref name="Soga-Japanese">[[:ja:蘇我氏|Soga clan]], Japanese Wikipedia.</ref> The two characters used in this name are [[:en:Ateji|Ateji]]; the meanings of the characters (蘇: "resuscitation"; 我: "self") are unrelated to the name meaning.
==Soga no Iname== [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] served as Great Minister from 536 until his death in 570, and was the first of the Soga clan to carry to extreme lengths the domination of the Throne by the nobility. One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}
==Family== *[[Takenouchi no Sukune]] (武内宿禰, ?–?) **[[Soga no Ishikawa]] (蘇我石川, ?–?) ***[[Soga no Machi]] (蘇我満智, ?–?) ****[[Soga no Karako]] (蘇我韓子, ?–465) *****[[:en:Soga no Koma|Soga no Koma]] (蘇我高麗, ?–?) ******[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] (蘇我稲目, c.506–570) *******[[Soga no Umako]] (蘇我馬子, 551?–626) ********[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] (蘇我蝦夷, 587–645) *********[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] (蘇我入鹿, 610?–645) ********[[Soga no Zentoko]] (蘇我善徳, ?–?) ********[[Soga no Kuramaro]] (蘇我倉麻呂, ?–?) *********[[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro]] (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂, ?–649) *********[[Soga no Akae]] (蘇我赤兄, 623?–?) *********[[Soga no Murajiko]] (蘇我連子, 611?–664) **********[[Soga no Yasumaro]] (蘇我安麻呂, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Himuka]] (蘇我日向, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Hatayasu]] (蘇我果安, ?–672)
==References== {{Reflist}} *Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Hall, John Whitney, et al. (1993). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: Volume 1 Ancient Japan''. Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}.]]

Emishi]] |dissolution =645 |ruled until =645, [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]] |cadet branches =Ishikawa clan }} The {{nihongo|'''Soga clan'''|{{linktext|蘇我|氏}}|Soga uji|lead=yes}} was one of the most powerful aristocratic kin groups [[Uji (clan)|(''uji'')]] of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] of the early Japanese state—the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato polity]]—and played a major role in the spread of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. Through the 5th and 7th centuries, the Soga monopolized the [[kabane]] or hereditary Rank of Great [[Omi (title)|Omi]] and was the first of many families to dominate the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] by influencing the order of succession and government policy.
The last Soga predates any historical work in Japan, and very little is known about its earliest members.
==Origins== The Soga clan is believed to have been founded by [[Soga no Ishikawa]], a great-grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]].
==Notation== Today, the name ''Soga'', when referring to the Soga clan, is written in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] as 蘇我. This notation derives from the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', where 蘇我 is the principal way in which this name is written.<ref name="nihonshoki-wiki">{{cite web |url=http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208102258/http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |title=Soga |archive-date=December 8, 2019 |website=The Nihon Shoki Wiki |accessdate=2023-07-01 |lang=en}}</ref> Other ways of writing the clan name appeared in other historical documents.<ref name="Soga-Japanese">[[:ja:蘇我氏|Soga clan]], Japanese Wikipedia.</ref> The two characters used in this name are [[:en:Ateji|Ateji]]; the meanings of the characters (蘇: "resuscitation"; 我: "self") are unrelated to the name meaning.
==Soga no Iname== [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] served as Great Minister from 536 until his death in 570, and was the first of the Soga clan to carry to extreme lengths the domination of the Throne by the nobility. One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}

==Origins== The Soga clan is believed to have been founded by [[Soga no Ishikawa]], a great-grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]].
==Notation== Today, the name ''Soga'', when referring to the Soga clan, is written in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] as 蘇我. This notation derives from the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', where 蘇我 is the principal way in which this name is written.<ref name="nihonshoki-wiki">{{cite web |url=http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208102258/http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |title=Soga |archive-date=December 8, 2019 |website=The Nihon Shoki Wiki |accessdate=2023-07-01 |lang=en}}</ref> Other ways of writing the clan name appeared in other historical documents.<ref name="Soga-Japanese">[[:ja:蘇我氏|Soga clan]], Japanese Wikipedia.</ref> The two characters used in this name are [[:en:Ateji|Ateji]]; the meanings of the characters (蘇: "resuscitation"; 我: "self") are unrelated to the name meaning.
==Soga no Iname== [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] served as Great Minister from 536 until his death in 570, and was the first of the Soga clan to carry to extreme lengths the domination of the Throne by the nobility. One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}
==Family== *[[Takenouchi no Sukune]] (武内宿禰, ?–?) **[[Soga no Ishikawa]] (蘇我石川, ?–?) ***[[Soga no Machi]] (蘇我満智, ?–?) ****[[Soga no Karako]] (蘇我韓子, ?–465) *****[[:en:Soga no Koma|Soga no Koma]] (蘇我高麗, ?–?) ******[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] (蘇我稲目, c.506–570) *******[[Soga no Umako]] (蘇我馬子, 551?–626) ********[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] (蘇我蝦夷, 587–645) *********[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] (蘇我入鹿, 610?–645) ********[[Soga no Zentoko]] (蘇我善徳, ?–?) ********[[Soga no Kuramaro]] (蘇我倉麻呂, ?–?) *********[[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro]] (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂, ?–649) *********[[Soga no Akae]] (蘇我赤兄, 623?–?) *********[[Soga no Murajiko]] (蘇我連子, 611?–664) **********[[Soga no Yasumaro]] (蘇我安麻呂, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Himuka]] (蘇我日向, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Hatayasu]] (蘇我果安, ?–672)
==References== {{Reflist}} *Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Hall, John Whitney, et al. (1993). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: Volume 1 Ancient Japan''. Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}.]]

=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] |titles =Various |parent house = [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] |founder = Soga no Ishikawa |final ruler =[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] |dissolution =645 |ruled until =645, [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]] |cadet branches =Ishikawa clan }} The {{nihongo|'''Soga clan'''|{{linktext|蘇我|氏}}|Soga uji|lead=yes}} was one of the most powerful aristocratic kin groups [[Uji (clan)|(''uji'')]] of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] of the early Japanese state—the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato polity]]—and played a major role in the spread of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. Through the 5th and 7th centuries, the Soga monopolized the [[kabane]] or hereditary Rank of Great [[Omi (title)|Omi]] and was the first of many families to dominate the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] by influencing the order of succession and government policy.
The last Soga predates any historical work in Japan, and very little is known about its earliest members.
==Origins== The Soga clan is believed to have been founded by [[Soga no Ishikawa]], a great-grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]].
==Notation== Today, the name ''Soga'', when referring to the Soga clan, is written in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] as 蘇我. This notation derives from the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', where 蘇我 is the principal way in which this name is written.<ref name="nihonshoki-wiki">{{cite web |url=http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208102258/http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |title=Soga |archive-date=December 8, 2019 |website=The Nihon Shoki Wiki |accessdate=2023-07-01 |lang=en}}</ref> Other ways of writing the clan name appeared in other historical documents.<ref name="Soga-Japanese">[[:ja:蘇我氏|Soga clan]], Japanese Wikipedia.</ref> The two characters used in this name are [[:en:Ateji|Ateji]]; the meanings of the characters (蘇: "resuscitation"; 我: "self") are unrelated to the name meaning.
==Soga no Iname== [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] served as Great Minister from 536 until his death in 570, and was the first of the Soga clan to carry to extreme lengths the domination of the Throne by the nobility. One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}

This notation derives from the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', where 蘇我 is the principal way in which this name is written.<ref name="nihonshoki-wiki">{{cite web |url=http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208102258/http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |title=Soga |archive-date=December 8, 2019 |website=The Nihon Shoki Wiki |accessdate=2023-07-01 |lang=en}}</ref> Other ways of writing the clan name appeared in other historical documents.<ref name="Soga-Japanese">[[:ja:蘇我氏|Soga clan]], Japanese Wikipedia.</ref> The two characters used in this name are [[:en:Ateji|Ateji]]; the meanings of the characters (蘇: "resuscitation"; 我: "self") are unrelated to the name meaning.
==Soga no Iname== [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] served as Great Minister from 536 until his death in 570, and was the first of the Soga clan to carry to extreme lengths the domination of the Throne by the nobility. One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}
==Family== *[[Takenouchi no Sukune]] (武内宿禰, ?–?) **[[Soga no Ishikawa]] (蘇我石川, ?–?) ***[[Soga no Machi]] (蘇我満智, ?–?) ****[[Soga no Karako]] (蘇我韓子, ?–465) *****[[:en:Soga no Koma|Soga no Koma]] (蘇我高麗, ?–?) ******[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] (蘇我稲目, c.506–570) *******[[Soga no Umako]] (蘇我馬子, 551?–626) ********[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] (蘇我蝦夷, 587–645) *********[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] (蘇我入鹿, 610?–645) ********[[Soga no Zentoko]] (蘇我善徳, ?–?) ********[[Soga no Kuramaro]] (蘇我倉麻呂, ?–?) *********[[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro]] (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂, ?–649) *********[[Soga no Akae]] (蘇我赤兄, 623?–?) *********[[Soga no Murajiko]] (蘇我連子, 611?–664) **********[[Soga no Yasumaro]] (蘇我安麻呂, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Himuka]] (蘇我日向, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Hatayasu]] (蘇我果安, ?–672)
==References== {{Reflist}} *Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Hall, John Whitney, et al. (1993). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: Volume 1 Ancient Japan''. Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}.]]

description|Aristocratic family (uji) in Yamato-period Japan (3rd-8th centuries)}} {{Distinguish |Soga clan (Sagami Province)}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 蘇我氏 |home province =[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] |titles =Various |parent house = [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] |founder = Soga no Ishikawa |final ruler =[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] |dissolution =645 |ruled until =645, [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]] |cadet branches =Ishikawa clan }} The {{nihongo|'''Soga clan'''|{{linktext|蘇我|氏}}|Soga uji|lead=yes}} was one of the most powerful aristocratic kin groups [[Uji (clan)|(''uji'')]] of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] of the early Japanese state—the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato polity]]—and played a major role in the spread of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. Through the 5th and 7th centuries, the Soga monopolized the [[kabane]] or hereditary Rank of Great [[Omi (title)|Omi]] and was the first of many families to dominate the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] by influencing the order of succession and government policy.
The last Soga predates any historical work in Japan, and very little is known about its earliest members.
==Origins== The Soga clan is believed to have been founded by [[Soga no Ishikawa]], a great-grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]].
==Notation== Today, the name ''Soga'', when referring to the Soga clan, is written in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] as 蘇我. This notation derives from the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', where 蘇我 is the principal way in which this name is written.<ref name="nihonshoki-wiki">{{cite web |url=http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208102258/http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |title=Soga |archive-date=December 8, 2019 |website=The Nihon Shoki Wiki |accessdate=2023-07-01 |lang=en}}</ref> Other ways of writing the clan name appeared in other historical documents.<ref name="Soga-Japanese">[[:ja:蘇我氏|Soga clan]], Japanese Wikipedia.</ref> The two characters used in this name are [[:en:Ateji|Ateji]]; the meanings of the characters (蘇: "resuscitation"; 我: "self") are unrelated to the name meaning.
==Soga no Iname== [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] served as Great Minister from 536 until his death in 570, and was the first of the Soga clan to carry to extreme lengths the domination of the Throne by the nobility. One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}
==Family== *[[Takenouchi no Sukune]] (武内宿禰, ?–?) **[[Soga no Ishikawa]] (蘇我石川, ?–?) ***[[Soga no Machi]] (蘇我満智, ?–?) ****[[Soga no Karako]] (蘇我韓子, ?–465) *****[[:en:Soga no Koma|Soga no Koma]] (蘇我高麗, ?–?) ******[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] (蘇我稲目, c.506–570) *******[[Soga no Umako]] (蘇我馬子, 551?–626) ********[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] (蘇我蝦夷, 587–645) *********[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] (蘇我入鹿, 610?–645) ********[[Soga no Zentoko]] (蘇我善徳, ?–?) ********[[Soga no Kuramaro]] (蘇我倉麻呂, ?–?) *********[[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro]] (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂, ?–649) *********[[Soga no Akae]] (蘇我赤兄, 623?–?) *********[[Soga no Murajiko]] (蘇我連子, 611?–664) **********[[Soga no Yasumaro]] (蘇我安麻呂, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Himuka]] (蘇我日向, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Hatayasu]] (蘇我果安, ?–672)
==References== {{Reflist}} *Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Hall, John Whitney, et al. (1993). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: Volume 1 Ancient Japan''. Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}.]]

One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}
==Family== *[[Takenouchi no Sukune]] (武内宿禰, ?–?) **[[Soga no Ishikawa]] (蘇我石川, ?–?) ***[[Soga no Machi]] (蘇我満智, ?–?) ****[[Soga no Karako]] (蘇我韓子, ?–465) *****[[:en:Soga no Koma|Soga no Koma]] (蘇我高麗, ?–?) ******[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] (蘇我稲目, c.506–570) *******[[Soga no Umako]] (蘇我馬子, 551?–626) ********[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] (蘇我蝦夷, 587–645) *********[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] (蘇我入鹿, 610?–645) ********[[Soga no Zentoko]] (蘇我善徳, ?–?) ********[[Soga no Kuramaro]] (蘇我倉麻呂, ?–?) *********[[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro]] (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂, ?–649) *********[[Soga no Akae]] (蘇我赤兄, 623?–?) *********[[Soga no Murajiko]] (蘇我連子, 611?–664) **********[[Soga no Yasumaro]] (蘇我安麻呂, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Himuka]] (蘇我日向, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Hatayasu]] (蘇我果安, ?–672)
==References== {{Reflist}} *Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Hall, John Whitney, et al. (1993). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: Volume 1 Ancient Japan''. Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}.]]

comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q909854]] Wikidata name: Soga clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2023}} {{Short description|Aristocratic family (uji) in Yamato-period Japan (3rd-8th centuries)}} {{Distinguish |Soga clan (Sagami Province)}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 蘇我氏 |home province =[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] |titles =Various |parent house = [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] |founder = Soga no Ishikawa |final ruler =[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] |dissolution =645 |ruled until =645, [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]] |cadet branches =Ishikawa clan }} The {{nihongo|'''Soga clan'''|{{linktext|蘇我|氏}}|Soga uji|lead=yes}} was one of the most powerful aristocratic kin groups [[Uji (clan)|(''uji'')]] of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] of the early Japanese state—the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato polity]]—and played a major role in the spread of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. Through the 5th and 7th centuries, the Soga monopolized the [[kabane]] or hereditary Rank of Great [[Omi (title)|Omi]] and was the first of many families to dominate the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] by influencing the order of succession and government policy.
The last Soga predates any historical work in Japan, and very little is known about its earliest members.
==Origins== The Soga clan is believed to have been founded by [[Soga no Ishikawa]], a great-grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]].
==Notation== Today, the name ''Soga'', when referring to the Soga clan, is written in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] as 蘇我. This notation derives from the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', where 蘇我 is the principal way in which this name is written.<ref name="nihonshoki-wiki">{{cite web |url=http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208102258/http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |title=Soga |archive-date=December 8, 2019 |website=The Nihon Shoki Wiki |accessdate=2023-07-01 |lang=en}}</ref> Other ways of writing the clan name appeared in other historical documents.<ref name="Soga-Japanese">[[:ja:蘇我氏|Soga clan]], Japanese Wikipedia.</ref> The two characters used in this name are [[:en:Ateji|Ateji]]; the meanings of the characters (蘇: "resuscitation"; 我: "self") are unrelated to the name meaning.
==Soga no Iname== [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] served as Great Minister from 536 until his death in 570, and was the first of the Soga clan to carry to extreme lengths the domination of the Throne by the nobility. One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}
==Family== *[[Takenouchi no Sukune]] (武内宿禰, ?–?) **[[Soga no Ishikawa]] (蘇我石川, ?–?) ***[[Soga no Machi]] (蘇我満智, ?–?) ****[[Soga no Karako]] (蘇我韓子, ?–465) *****[[:en:Soga no Koma|Soga no Koma]] (蘇我高麗, ?–?) ******[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] (蘇我稲目, c.506–570) *******[[Soga no Umako]] (蘇我馬子, 551?–626) ********[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] (蘇我蝦夷, 587–645) *********[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] (蘇我入鹿, 610?–645) ********[[Soga no Zentoko]] (蘇我善徳, ?–?) ********[[Soga no Kuramaro]] (蘇我倉麻呂, ?–?) *********[[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro]] (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂, ?–649) *********[[Soga no Akae]] (蘇我赤兄, 623?–?) *********[[Soga no Murajiko]] (蘇我連子, 611?–664) **********[[Soga no Yasumaro]] (蘇我安麻呂, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Himuka]] (蘇我日向, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Hatayasu]] (蘇我果安, ?–672)
==References== {{Reflist}} *Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Hall, John Whitney, et al. (1993). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: Volume 1 Ancient Japan''. Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}.]]

They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}
==Family== *[[Takenouchi no Sukune]] (武内宿禰, ?–?) **[[Soga no Ishikawa]] (蘇我石川, ?–?) ***[[Soga no Machi]] (蘇我満智, ?–?) ****[[Soga no Karako]] (蘇我韓子, ?–465) *****[[:en:Soga no Koma|Soga no Koma]] (蘇我高麗, ?–?) ******[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] (蘇我稲目, c.506–570) *******[[Soga no Umako]] (蘇我馬子, 551?–626) ********[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] (蘇我蝦夷, 587–645) *********[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] (蘇我入鹿, 610?–645) ********[[Soga no Zentoko]] (蘇我善徳, ?–?) ********[[Soga no Kuramaro]] (蘇我倉麻呂, ?–?) *********[[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro]] (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂, ?–649) *********[[Soga no Akae]] (蘇我赤兄, 623?–?) *********[[Soga no Murajiko]] (蘇我連子, 611?–664) **********[[Soga no Yasumaro]] (蘇我安麻呂, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Himuka]] (蘇我日向, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Hatayasu]] (蘇我果安, ?–672)
==References== {{Reflist}} *Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Hall, John Whitney, et al. (1993). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: Volume 1 Ancient Japan''. Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}.]]
[[en:Soga clan]]
<!-- Bot generated title --><!-- A Japan Times article would not be the best source in the world for this -- they have published articles that, for instance, misspelled Nakanishi Susumu's name and called the Man'youshuu Japanese "oldest collection of domestic poetry" -- but the fact that what is actually linked is a blog, even one by the excellent Aileen Kawagoe, is concerning. ~Hijiri88, August 2015. --> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:سوغا (عشيرة)]] [[ca:Clan Soga]] [[de:Soga (Klan)]] [[es:Clan Soga]] [[fa:خاندان سوگا]] [[fi:Soga-klaani]] [[fr:Clan Soga]] [[hr:Soga (klan)]] [[hu:Szoga klán]] [[id:Klan Soga]] [[it:Clan Soga]] [[ja:蘇我氏]] [[ko:소가씨]] [[nl:Soga]] [[pl:Ród Soga]] [[pt:Clã Soga]] [[ru:Сога (род)]] [[sh:Soga (klan)]] [[tr:Soga boyu]] [[uk:Рід Соґа]] [[vi:Gia tộc Soga]] [[zh:蘇我氏]] {{wikidata link|Q909854}} [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:Soga clan| ]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q909854]] Wikidata name: Soga clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:30, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2023}} {{Short description|Aristocratic family (uji) in Yamato-period Japan (3rd-8th centuries)}} {{Distinguish |Soga clan (Sagami Province)}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname nihongo = 蘇我氏 |home province =[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] |titles =Various |parent house = [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] |founder = Soga no Ishikawa |final ruler =[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] |dissolution =645 |ruled until =645, [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]] |cadet branches =Ishikawa clan }} The {{nihongo|'''Soga clan'''|{{linktext|蘇我|氏}}|Soga uji|lead=yes}} was one of the most powerful aristocratic kin groups [[Uji (clan)|(''uji'')]] of the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] of the early Japanese state—the [[:en:Yamato period|Yamato polity]]—and played a major role in the spread of [[:en:Buddhism in Japan|Buddhism in Japan]]. Through the 5th and 7th centuries, the Soga monopolized the [[kabane]] or hereditary Rank of Great [[Omi (title)|Omi]] and was the first of many families to dominate the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]] by influencing the order of succession and government policy.
The last Soga predates any historical work in Japan, and very little is known about its earliest members.
==Origins== The Soga clan is believed to have been founded by [[Soga no Ishikawa]], a great-grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kōgen|Emperor Kōgen]].
==Notation== Today, the name ''Soga'', when referring to the Soga clan, is written in [[:en:Kanji|Kanji]] as 蘇我. This notation derives from the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', where 蘇我 is the principal way in which this name is written.<ref name="nihonshoki-wiki">{{cite web |url=http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191208102258/http://nihonshoki.wikidot.com/search:site/q/soga |title=Soga |archive-date=December 8, 2019 |website=The Nihon Shoki Wiki |accessdate=2023-07-01 |lang=en}}</ref> Other ways of writing the clan name appeared in other historical documents.<ref name="Soga-Japanese">[[:ja:蘇我氏|Soga clan]], Japanese Wikipedia.</ref> The two characters used in this name are [[:en:Ateji|Ateji]]; the meanings of the characters (蘇: "resuscitation"; 我: "self") are unrelated to the name meaning.
==Soga no Iname== [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] served as Great Minister from 536 until his death in 570, and was the first of the Soga clan to carry to extreme lengths the domination of the Throne by the nobility. One of the chief ways he exerted influence was through marital connections with the imperial family; Iname married two of his daughters to [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]], one giving offspring to an Emperor, [[:en:Emperor Yōmei|Emperor Yōmei]]. The next five emperors all had a wife or mother who was a descendant of Iname. In this way the Soga unified and strengthened the country by expanding the power of the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]] as a symbol and spiritual leader as they took control of secular matters.
==Connection to Buddhism from Korea and China== The Soga clan had much contact with foreigners, including the Koreans and the Chinese. They favored the adoption of Buddhism and of governmental and cultural models based on Chinese [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]].<ref>{{cite web | url=http://countrystudies.us/japan/6.htm | title=Japan - KOFUN AND ASUKA PERIODS }}</ref>
The Soga clan supported the spread of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] when it was first introduced in Japan during the 6th century by monks from [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (Japanese ''Kudara'').<ref>[http://www.pref.nara.jp/pro-e/rekishi.htm History of Nara]</ref> Many Japanese at the time, disliking foreign ideas and believing that this new religion might be an affront to the traditional "[[kami]]" or spirits and gods, opposed Buddhism. The rival [[Mononobe clan|Mononobe]] and [[Nakatomi clan|Nakatomi]] clans succeeded in gathering hostility against this new religion when a disease spread, following the arrival of a Buddhist statue. It was claimed the epidemic was a sign of anger by the local spirits and the Soga temple at the palace was burned down.
The Soga family, however, firmly believed that the most civilized people believed in Buddhism and continued to actively promote it, placing a holy image of the [[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] in a major [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]. [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] claimed that [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] brought with it a new form of government that would subvert the independence of the clans, unifying the people under the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor]]. After fifty years of ideological war, Buddhism, defended and protected by the Soga, began to take hold in Japan.
==Political assertiveness and reactions== By 644, the heads of the Soga were no longer satisfied to act behind the scenes. [[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] and his son [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] began to build increasingly elaborate palaces and tombs for themselves, styling themselves "sovereigns".
In response, the leader of the [[Nakatomi clan]], Nakatomi no Kamatari (later known as the founder of the [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] and traditionally referred to as [[:en:Fujiwara no Kamatari|Fujiwara no Kamatari]]), conspired with [[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro]] and Prince Naka no Ōe (later [[:en:Emperor Tenji|Emperor Tenji]]) and arranged for Iruka's assassination. Prince Ōe himself attacked Iruka during a court ceremony concerning edicts from Korean kingdoms in front of [[:en:Empress Kōgyoku|Empress Kōgyoku]]; he survived, but the Empress left the scene and Ōe's guards finished Iruka off. Subsequently, Soga no Emishi committed suicide by burning down his own residence, destroying many important court documents. Soga followers were dispersed and even killed; the Empress abdicated and her brother took the throne as [[Emperor Kōtoku]]. The Soga clan's hold over the imperial family was broken and two years later the Emperor enacted the [[Taika Reform]], returning full power to the emperor. This disruptive and transformative event is known as the [[:en:Isshi incident|Isshi incident]].<ref name=PF>{{cite book|last=Ponsonby-Fane|first=Richard|title=The Imperial House of Japan|year=1959|publisher=Ponsonby Memorial Society|location=Kyoto|pages=49–50}}</ref>
==Legacy== In 2005, the remains of a building which may have been [[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]]'s residence were discovered in [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara]]. This discovery appeared to be consistent with the description found in ''Nihon Shoki''.<ref>[http://heritageofjapan.wordpress.com/inception-of-the-imperial-system-asuka-era/the-ruins-of-asuka-yamato/preserving-ruins-of-an-ancient-era-asukamura-maintains-cultural-heritage-while-looking-to-the-future/in-the-news-soga-house-believed-found/ "Soga no Iruka house believed found,"] ''Japan Times Weekly,'' 14 November 2005; retrieved 2013-2-29.</ref>{{better source|date=August 2015}}
==Family== *[[Takenouchi no Sukune]] (武内宿禰, ?–?) **[[Soga no Ishikawa]] (蘇我石川, ?–?) ***[[Soga no Machi]] (蘇我満智, ?–?) ****[[Soga no Karako]] (蘇我韓子, ?–465) *****[[:en:Soga no Koma|Soga no Koma]] (蘇我高麗, ?–?) ******[[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] (蘇我稲目, c.506–570) *******[[Soga no Umako]] (蘇我馬子, 551?–626) ********[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] (蘇我蝦夷, 587–645) *********[[:en:Soga no Iruka|Soga no Iruka]] (蘇我入鹿, 610?–645) ********[[Soga no Zentoko]] (蘇我善徳, ?–?) ********[[Soga no Kuramaro]] (蘇我倉麻呂, ?–?) *********[[:en:Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawamaro]] (蘇我倉山田石川麻呂, ?–649) *********[[Soga no Akae]] (蘇我赤兄, 623?–?) *********[[Soga no Murajiko]] (蘇我連子, 611?–664) **********[[Soga no Yasumaro]] (蘇我安麻呂, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Himuka]] (蘇我日向, ?–?) *********[[Soga no Hatayasu]] (蘇我果安, ?–672)
==References== {{Reflist}} *Sansom, George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334''' Stanford, California: Stanford University Press. *Hall, John Whitney, et al. (1993). ''The Cambridge History of Japan: Volume 1 Ancient Japan''. Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}.]]
